coredata.dc:title: Effects of gas-liquid interface on the theoretical X-ray absorption spectroscopy of phenylenediamines
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shaofeng
Zhong, Xiaoxia
Guo, Ying
Shi, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we performed molecular dynamics simulations for m-, o-, and p-phenylenediamine both in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface. Polar-angle distributions were obtained to characterize the molecular orientation. Theoretical X-ray absorption spectra were calculated to evaluate the effects of the aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface on the electronic structures of phenylenediamines using density functional theory. The bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface environments allow transitions that are forbidden for isolated p- and m-phenylenediamine. The spectra sampled at different polar angles show diverse lineshapes, which are sensitive to the microenvironment. The histogram-based gradient boosting regression tree was used to obtain the center line of spectra group consisting of 100 sampled spectra for each isomer in the bulk aqueous solution or at the gas–liquid interface. Several machine learning classifiers were further applied to classify the bulk aqueous solutions and gas–liquid interfaces. This study provides insight into the molecular dynamics at the gas–liquid interface and the aqueous solution effects on the molecular electronic structures. These theoretical X-ray absorption spectra are necessary for the assignment of experimental X-ray absorption spectroscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MD
DFT
Phenylenediamines
X-ray absorption spectroscopy
Gas–liquid interface

We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structures, atomic indexes, and orientation vectors of pPD, mPD, and oPD.
X-ray absorption spectra of isolated pPD, mPD, oPD at PBE/Def2TZVPP level of theory.
Statistical frequency distribution histograms of pPD, mPD, and oPD.
The X-ray absorption spectra from 1N(1s) of pPD, mPD and oPD.
The X-ray absorption spectra from 3C(1s) of pPD, mPD and oPD.

Excitation energies (labeled as ΔE in eV) and oscillator strength (labeled as OS) of the first four transitions of pPD, mPD, oPD. The first column labels “(1s → 30, 1s → 31, 1s → 32, 1s → 33)” refer to the first to the fourth virtual orbitals, respectively.
Mean accuracy of the Gaussian processes classifier for pPD, mPD, and oPD.

head.ce:title: Effects of gas-liquid interface on the theoretical X-ray absorption spectroscopy of phenylenediamines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Member of Magnetic Confinement Fusion Research Center, Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Shanghai 201620, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Member of Magnetic Confinement Fusion Research Center
Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Member of Magnetic Confinement Fusion Research Center, Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Shanghai 201620, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Astronomy, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Astronomy, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we performed molecular dynamics simulations for m-, o-, and p-phenylenediamine both in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface. Polar-angle distributions were obtained to characterize the molecular orientation. Theoretical X-ray absorption spectra were calculated to evaluate the effects of the aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface on the electronic structures of phenylenediamines using density functional theory. The bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface environments allow transitions that are forbidden for isolated p- and m-phenylenediamine. The spectra sampled at different polar angles show diverse lineshapes, which are sensitive to the microenvironment. The histogram-based gradient boosting regression tree was used to obtain the center line of spectra group consisting of 100 sampled spectra for each isomer in the bulk aqueous solution or at the gas–liquid interface. Several machine learning classifiers were further applied to classify the bulk aqueous solutions and gas–liquid interfaces. This study provides insight into the molecular dynamics at the gas–liquid interface and the aqueous solution effects on the molecular electronic structures. These theoretical X-ray absorption spectra are necessary for the assignment of experimental X-ray absorption spectroscopy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution is an important environment where several chemical reactions typically occur, e.g., advanced oxidation processes (AOP) in wastewater treatment. In particular, the gas–liquid interface accelerates the reactions of organic compounds [1]. According to frontier orbital theory, the electronic structures of chemical compounds determine the mechanisms of reactions. Quantum chemistry calculations indicate that additional water molecules increase the rate constant and lower the reaction energy barrier of the potential energy surface [2–4]. Therefore, elucidating the effects of both the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface on the electronic structures of molecules is important for understanding the chemical reactions in a gas–liquid two phase reactive system. However, it is difficult to study the aqueous solution effects on the electronic structures of molecules. Photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy are powerful tools for investigating the interface properties [5,6]. In photoelectron spectroscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) experiments on molecular beams and micro-liquid jets, molecules are excited by electrons or photons. Due to the limitations of theoretical methods and the sensitivity of excited electronic configurations to the microenvironments, a systematic disparity exists between the experimental and calculated vertical detachment energy and transition energies [7,8].

As a type of laboratory two-phase reactive system, atmospheric pressure plasma has numerous applications in organic chemical synthesis [9] and the degradations of organic micropollutants (OMPs) in wastewater treatment [10–13]. Phenylenediamines act as important redox-active ligands and precursors [14,15], and the three isomers, i.e., o-phenylenediamine (oPD), m-phenylenediamine (mPD), and p-phenylenediamine (pPD)], treated by the atmospheric pressure plasmas, are all able to produce carbon quantum dots [16,17]. For example, Ji et al. used aqueous solution phase methods to synthesize various near infrared (NIR) carbon dots (C-dots) from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) [18]. mPD and oPD also are used as a building block for polyimide battery cathode materials [19,20]. Especially, hazardous compound pPD used in hair dye is a harmful water pollutant to human health and the environment [21].

Despite the studies mentioned above, there have been only a few atomic-scale experimental and theoretical investigations of phenylenediamines. The physical–chemical processes involved in the treatment of phenylenediamines in two-phase reactive systems are poorly understood. One important question is where the radicals induced by gaseous phase atmospheric pressure plasma come into contact and collide with organic compounds in the bulk aqueous solution. Specifically, atomic scale molecular dynamics and properties of phenylenediamines at the gas–liquid interface, are important for understanding the mass transfer and chemical reactions in two-phase reactive systems.

In this study, we performed molecular dynamics simulations to understand the dynamics of three phenylenediamine isomers in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface. To characterize the influences of water molecules on the electronic structures of phenylenediamines, we conducted the theoretical XAS based on density functional theory. Our calculations showed that equivalent atoms in isolated phenylenediamines are not equivalent in bulk solution due to the drastic changes in the molecular geometries and local chemical environments. The molecular orientation is macroscopically isotropic in bulk aqueous solution but is enhanced at specific polar angle at the gas–liquid interface. The spectra suggest that the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface environments allow transitions that are forbidden for isolated p- and m-phenylenediamine. The machine learning method, i.e., histogram-based gradient boosting regression tree was used to obtain the center lines i.e. averaged spectral lines of hundreds of sampled spectra. Several machine learning classifiers, e.g., Gaussian processes classifier, were applied to classify the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface. This study provides insights into the spatial distributions of phenylenediamines at the gas–liquid interface and the basic data for assigning the spectra for experimental X-ray absorption spectra in the gaseous phase, bulk aqueous solution, and at the gas–liquid interface.

The molecular dynamics simulations (MDs) were performed using the CP2K package [22] based on classical force fields produced by AmberTools22 [23]. The timestep was fixed at 0.5 fs. After MD simulation for 500 ps under the NPT ensemble employing an ambient pressure of 1 atm and a temperature of 300 K, the average sizes of the cubic box were 35.25463, 35.32423 and 35.26593 Å3 for pPD, mPD and oPD, respectively. Then, we performed MD simulation for 1.5 ns under the NVT ensemble using the aforementioned averaged box sizes and periodic boundary conditions to achieve thermal equilibrium bulk aqueous solution systems. We continued performing MD simulation for another 5 ns, and sampled the frames every 100 timesteps i.e. 50 fs. Based on the equilibrium bulk aqueous solution systems, we built the gas–liquid interface systems by adding two vacuum layers along the z-axis, and extended the box sizes to 35.25462 × 105.7638, 35.32422 × 105.9726, 35.26592 × 105.7977 Å3 for pPD, mPD and oPD, respectively. Snapshots of the initial gas–liquid interface systems are shown in Fig. S2. Similar to the bulk aqueous solution systems, we ran MD simulation for 1.5 ns to equilibrate the gas–liquid interface systems and ran MD simulation for 5 ns to sample frames every 50 fs.

X-ray absorption spectra were also calculated using CP2K following the approach described in [24]. Figs. S3-S10 illustrate the effect of the chosen basis sets on X-ray absorption spectra of pPD using PBE density functional and Def2TZVPP, 6-311G**, cc-pVTZ [25], cc-PVDZ[26], and def2-SVP basis sets. The 2-zeta basis sets def2-SVP and cc-PVDZ overestimated the virtual orbital energy levels, while the 3-zeta basis sets 6-311G**, cc-PVTZ, and def2-TZVPP gave similar results for the low-energy band. For higher bands, Def2TZVPP gave the lowest virtual orbital energy levels while 6-311G** gave the highest virtual orbital energy levels. Considering the PBE/Def2TZVPP level of theory gave the results close to experimental results for phenol [27,28], we continue using Def2TZVPP and 6-311G** basis sets for the following calculations.

For the isolated molecules, i.e., the gaseous phase, their X-ray absorption spectra were calculated at PBE/Def2TZVPP level of theory. For the bulk aqueous solution systems and the gas–liquid interface systems, the following hybrid basis sets were employed. Def2TZVPP basis sets [29,30] were employed on N, C, and H atoms of phenylenediamines, and 6-311G** basis sets [31,32] were employed on O and H of water molecules. The VMD and ASE packages were used to visualize trajectories and rebuild the systems [33,34]. Machine learning was carried out using the scikit-learn package [35].

First, we calculated the X-ray absorption spectra of isolated molecules, i.e., the gaseous phase. The molecular structures, and atomic indices are shown in Fig. 1 . The results of isolated molecules were used here to assess the basis sets and compare with those in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface. These results also provide essential theoretical information for potential (albeit still unavailable) micro molecular jet experiments using X-ray absorption spectroscopy.

Fig. 2 shows the X-ray absorption spectra of isolated pPD, mPD, and oPD at the PBE/Def2TZVPP level of theory. These molecules have C2h, C2, and C2 point groups, respectively. The equivalent atoms give the same X-ray absorption signals, as proven by the current calculations. Therefore, Fig. 2 only shows the spectra of 3C, 5C and 1N for pPD, 3C, 5C, 6C, 8C, and 1N for mPD and 3C, 5C, 7C, and 1N for oPD in Fig. 2. The main transitions contributing to the spectral peaks are labeled in Fig. 2. 3Cs of pPD, mPD, and oPD are carbons directly bonded to nitrogen atoms, and thus are the most affected by NH2 groups. The peaks’ locations of 3Cs are higher than those of the other carbon atoms, indicating that the nitrogen atoms lower the 1s energy levels of 3Cs. Around 290 eV, the absorptions from 3Cs are very weak, while other carbon atoms have additional absorption peaks due to the transitions from 1s to 34 or 33 virtual orbitals. As the two NH2 groups come close to each other, the oscillator strengths of the first peaks from 1N(1s) of pPD, mPD and oPD decrease successively, while the oscillator strengths of third peaks increases successively due to the number of contributing transitions.

The spectra were convolved by a gaussian function, which are described in the Supplementary Material. Therefore, convolved curves didn’t present all the details; we provide Table 1 to list the excitation energies and oscillator strengths of the first four transitions from 1N (1s) and 3C (1s), and other data are listed in Tables S1-S3. For all three isomers, the maximum oscillator strengths originate from the transitions from 1N(1s) to the fourth virtual orbitals. However, the 3C atom connected to the 1N atom is affected by molecular symmetry. For mPD and oPD, the transitions from 3C (1s) to the first virtual orbital i.e., the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), give the maximum oscillator strength, but this transition is forbidden for pPD. Instead, the maximum oscillator strength for pPD is attributed to the transition from 3C(1s) to the second virtual orbital. In addition, for mPD, the transition from 8C(1s) to the second virtual orbital is forbidden, and its oscillator strength is listed in Table S3. An intuitive understanding is that the first virtual orbital (MO 30) of pPD is not located at 3C shown in Fig. S11 and the second virtual orbital (MO 31) of mPD is not located at 8C shown in Fig. S12. However, these two forbidden transitions become possible in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface, which will be discussed in bellow.

Molecules in the bulk aqueous solutions have macroscopically isotropic orientations. The vectors from 3C to 4C, 7C to 4C, and 7C to 3C shown in Fig. 1 were used to characterize the orientations of pPD, mPD, and oPD, respectively. The polar angle
θ
is defined as the angle between the aforementioned vector and z-axis. For an isotropic vector, its probability in stereoangle
d Ω
is
P =
d Ω
4 π
=
s i n θ d θ d φ
4 π
. Integrating with respect to azimuth
φ
, we obtain the probability in polar angle
θ
,
P =
s i n θ
2
d θ
; therefore, the probability density function is written as:
(1)
ρ
θ
=
s i n θ
2
Fig. S13 shows the frequency distribution histograms, which fit the Equation (1) very well. These angular frequency distribution histograms will be compared to results of the gas–liquid interface.

Limited by computing resources and time, X-ray absorption spectra calculations were performed on a total of 600 typical frames that were picked based on the frequency distribution histograms. Subsystems, including the pPD, mPD, oPD, and their surrounding 64 water molecules, were used to approximate the aqueous effect on the organic molecules. The reasons and considerations are as follows.

(1) Calculating the X-ray absorption spectrum for every frame in MD trajectories would be perfect and could provide the most abundant information. Unfortunately, it is impossible to calculate the X-ray absorption spectra for whole systems based on the whole electron wave function due to the extremely large requirements of computing resources. (2) The size of subsystems is determined based on the radial distribution functions of oxygen atoms in water molecule and first nitrogen atom in pPD, mPD, and oPD as shown in Fig. S 14. The first solvent shells around the 1N are located at 3.45 Å and include three water molecules, the second solvent shells are located at 7.05, 7.65, 7.05 Å and include 47, 62, and 47 water molecules, respectively. (3) We checked the effect of the number of water molecules on the XAS in Fig. S 15 for pPD considering 8, 12, 32, 64, 80 and water molecules.

The subsystems were extracted from the trajectories using nearest neighbor lists in two steps. (1) We calculated the distances DOC between the oxygen atom in the water molecule and the center of mass of phenylenediamine (pPD, mPD, oPD). Then, we saved the index of the oxygen atom and distance into a dictionary data structure for the nearest neighbor lists. (2) We sorted the dictionary by DOC, and picked the nearest 64 water molecules to build subsystem.

Compared with the bulk aqueous solutions, the gas–liquid interfaces are macroscopically asymmetric along the z-axis, resulting in anisotropic orientations of the phenylenediamine molecules. In addition, the gas–liquid interface is not smooth mirror plane but a thermodynamically kinetic surface. The phenylenediamines “dance” at the gas–liquid interface and have specific orientations.

Fig. 3 shows the statistical frequency distribution histograms of the polar angle of pPD, mPD and oPD defined the same as the polar angle for the bulk aqueous solution shown in Fig. 1. Obviously, statistical frequency distribution histograms are different from
ρ =
s i n θ
2
and the distributions of bulk aqueous solutions. We used the Gaussian process regressor (a machine learning method) to fit the frequency distribution histograms to obtain smooth functions.

The competition between two NH2 groups leads to the vector of pPD being enhanced at the angle
θ =
π 2
, indicating that the line connecting the two NH2 groups of pPD is parallel to the gas–liquid interface. In addition, the vector from 7C to 5C is isotropic, which is shown in Fig. S16. mPD, oPD have C2 point group, therefore, nitrogen atoms are equivalent, but the four carbon atoms not directly bounded to nitrogen are not all equivalent. The frequency distribution histograms of mPD and oPD shift to larger angles, indicating that NH2 groups preferentially remain in the liquid phase and the aromatic ring remains in the gaseous phase. A simple ab initio calculation at M062X/Def2TZVPP level of theory indicates a tendency of hydrogen bonds between H2O and NH2 group shown by Fig. S17. The most probable angles of the vector in mPD and oPD are
θ = 1.932 ,
1.963
rad, respectively.

Based on the statistical frequency distribution histograms, we extracted subsystems for pPD, mPD and oPD to calculate their X-ray absorption spectra shown in Fig. 4 . Here we discuss the results of the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface.

The macro solution is uniform at the macro spatial–temporal scale. In contrast, on the micro spatial–temporal scale, the chemical environment of the molecules changed dramatically. Besides the rotations and vibrations of isolated molecules, that are affected by the microenvironment, the molecular geometries of pPD, mPD, and oPD vary over time. X-ray absorption spectra reflect directly effects of the microenvironment. One important effect of water molecules on molecular electronic structures of phenylenediamines is that the forbidden transitions from 3C(1s) to the first virtual orbital of pPD and from 8C(1s) to the second virtual orbital of mPD are enabled. The corresponding oscillator strengths are shown in Figs. S18 and S19. The bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface both lower the energies of the virtual orbitals, i.e., lower the excitation energies. For instance, the chemical shifts of excitation energies from 1N(1s) to the first virtual orbitals due to the bulk aqueous solution compared the gaseous phase are estimated as −1.54, −1.79, and −1.87 eV. The gas–liquid interface induces the chemical shifts of −0.97, −1.05, and −0.94 eV. Chemical shifts of transitions from 1s to higher virtual orbitals are much larger, as shown in Tables S5-S10.

To distinguish quantitatively the effects of the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface, the machine learning method histogram-based gradient boosting regression tree was used to extract and recognize the boundary and the center line of the XAS group consisting of 100 spectral lines for each isomer in the bulk solutions or at the gas–liquid interface. Here, the X-ray absorption spectra of subsystems are not equal-weighted. The frequency histograms obtained in Fig. 3 were used as weights for every X-ray absorption spectrum. The predictions, i.e. center lines, intuitively and significantly illustrate the difference between the effects of the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface on X-ray absorption spectra of 1N (Fig. 4) and 3C directly linked to 1N (Fig. 5 ). The spectra of 2N, 4C, 5C, 6C, 7C and 8C are shown in Fig. S68, Figs. S70-S74.

Compared with the spectra of isolated molecules, the number of peaks in spectra of the bulk aqueous solutions and the gas–liquid interfaces are reduced. The spectra of oPD at the gas–liquid interface shows a broader peak than those of mPD and pPD around 404.7 eV. The FWHM of the spectra of oPD, mPD and pPD are 2.47, 1.43 and 1.11 eV, respectively, which are obtained by fitting the three Gaussian functions shown in Fig. S75. Double Gaussian functions were used to fit the spectra from 3C(1s) of pPD, mPD, and oPD shown in Fig. S76. The FHWMs are listed in Table S11. The oPD still shows a broader first peak than mPD and oPD. The spectra from 5C, 6C, 7C, 8C shows more complex structures than those from 3C and 4C. The center lines of 5C, 6C, 7C, 8C need more Gaussian functions to fit as shown in Figs. S77-S80.

The water molecules surrounding phenylenediamines restrict the space for distribution of virtual orbitals. Meanwhile, the long-range weak interactions exist between the phenylenediamines and its surrounding water molecules. In this work, we used DFT-D3 to improve the calculations of energy levels for PBE density functional. The combination of physical and chemical factors modifies the spatial distribution of virtual orbitals. As consequences, the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface both reduce the energy level spacings of virtual orbitals. Although the oscillator strengths of transitions of molecules in the bulk aqueous solutions and the gas–liquid are smaller than those of isolated molecules. However, the transition lines are closer. Therefore, the oscillator strengths of main peaks in some convolved spectra of bulk aqueous solutions and gas–liquid interfaces are greater than those of isolated molecules in the gaseous phase, which are shown in Figs. S20-S67 (where the yellow lines are spectra of isolated molecules in the gaseous phase).

The spectra are sensitive to microenvironments: the subsystems at different sampled polar angles have diverse microenvironments, and thus return spectra of diverse lineshapes compared with isolated molecules. Two hundred spectra of one isomer in the bulk solution and at the gas–liquid overlapped, making it difficult to distinguish the spectra of the bulk aqueous solutions and the gas–liquid interfaces. We tested several machine learning classifiers applied to the datasets, an example of which is the high dimensional vector composed of the oscillation strengthens of 60 transitions. The label is assigned as 1 if the spectrum comes from the gas–liquid interface. For each isomer, e.g., pPD, the training set has 160 labeled examples, while the testing set has 40 labeled examples. Table 2 lists the mean accuracy of the Gaussian processes classifier on the given test data and labels. It is obvious that the predicting accuracy is different on different isomers and atomic sites. But in all cases, the accuracy is greater than 50%. For 8C, the Gaussian processes classifier was excellent: its mean accuracies for the three isomers are greater than 85%. The mean accuracies of other methods, e.g., Nearest Neighbors, and Linear SVM, are listed in Tables S12-S14.

To study the effects of two-phase aqueous environment on the electronic structures of micro organic molecules, we performed molecular dynamics simulations of p-, m-, and o-phenylenediamine in the bulk aqueous solution and at the gas–liquid interface. Molecules in the bulk aqueous solution have macroscopically isotropic orientations. In contrast, molecules at the gas–liquid interface have macroscopically anisotropic orientations that also depend on the symmetry of the isomer. Theoretical X-ray absorption spectroscopy was used to characterize the aqueous environments on the electronic structure of phenylenediamines using density function theory. The machine learning method histogram-based gradient boosting regression tree with weighting succeeded in illustrating the boundary and centerline of the spectra groups consisting of 100 spectral lines for each isomer in the bulk aqueous or at the gas–liquid interfaces. Furthermore, several machine learning classifiers were applied to classify spectra of the bulk aqueous solution and gas–liquid interface. This study provides insight into the molecular electronic structures at the gas–liquid interface and the bulk aqueous solution. These theoretical spectra of gaseous phase, bulk aqueous solution phase, and gas–liquid interface phase should provide important guidance for assigning the possible experimental X-ray absorption spectra.

Shaofeng Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – Original. Xiaoxia Zhong: Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition. Ying Guo: Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition. Jianjun Shi: Reviewing and Editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 12175141, 11875104, 12175036, 12205041), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (Grant No. 21ZR1435200). We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.156675.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photochemical reaction of phenol becomes ultrafast at the air-water interface

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kusaka, S. Nihonyanagi, and T. Tahara, The photochemical reaction of phenol becomes ultrafast at the air-water interface. Nature Chemistry, 2021. 13(4): p. 306-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prediction of reaction mechanism for OH radical-mediated phenol oxidation using quantum chemical calculation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Dwinandha, B. Zhang, and M. Fujii, Prediction of reaction mechanism for OH radical-mediated phenol oxidation using quantum chemical calculation. Chemosphere, 2022. 291: p. 132763.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The mechanism and kinetics model of degradation of dicarboxylic acids by hydroxyl radicals under atmospheric conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yu, L. Pan, H. Xiong, X. Xie, Q. Zhang, Q. Sun, et al., The Mechanism and Kinetics Model of Degradation of Dicarboxylic Acids by Hydroxyl Radicals under Atmospheric Conditions. The Journal of Physical Chemistry A, 2022. 126(5): p. 787-799.
M. F. Ruiz-Lopez, J. S. Francisco, M. T. C. Martins-Costa, and J. M. Anglada, Molecular reactions at aqueous interfaces. Nature Reviews Chemistry, 2020. 4(9): p. 459-475.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combining advanced photoelectron spectroscopy approaches to analyse deeply buried GaP(As)/Si(100) interfaces: Interfacial chemical states and complete band energy diagrams

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Romanyuk, A. Paszuk, I. Gordeev, R. G. Wilks, S. Ueda, C. Hartmann, et al., Combining advanced photoelectron spectroscopy approaches to analyse deeply buried GaP(As)/Si(100) interfaces: Interfacial chemical states and complete band energy diagrams. Applied Surface Science, 2022: p. 154630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Local atomic structure of the GaN-side of the Al2O3/GaN interface revealed by X-ray absorption spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Isomura, D. Kikuta, N. Takahashi, S. Kosaka, and K. Kataoka, Local atomic structure of the GaN-side of the Al2O3/GaN interface revealed by X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Applied Surface Science, 2020. 515: p. 146058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physical properties, exciton analysis, and visualization of core-excited states: an intermediate state representation approach

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wenzel and A. Dreuw, Physical Properties, Exciton Analysis, and Visualization of Core-Excited States: An Intermediate State Representation Approach. Journal of Chemical Theory and Computation, 2016. 12(3): p. 1314-1330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unravelling the role of an aqueous environment on the electronic structure and ionization of phenol using photoelectron spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. W. Riley, B. Wang, J. L. Woodhouse, M. Assmann, G. A. Worth, and H. H. Fielding, Unravelling the Role of an Aqueous Environment on the Electronic Structure and Ionization of Phenol Using Photoelectron Spectroscopy. The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters, 2018. 9(4): p. 678-682.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microplasmas for advanced materials and devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. H. Chiang, D. Mariotti, R. M. Sankaran, J. G. Eden, and K. K. Ostrikov, Microplasmas for Advanced Materials and Devices. Adv Mater, 2020. 32(18): p. e1905508.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on the treatment of organic wastewater by discharge plasma combined with oxidants and catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Fan, H. Wu, R. Liu, L. Meng, and Y. Sun, Review on the treatment of organic wastewater by discharge plasma combined with oxidants and catalysts. Environ Sci Pollut Res Int, 2021. 28(3): p. 2522-2548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-thermal plasma as a promising route for the removal of tar from the product gas of biomass gasification – a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Saleem, J. Harris, K. Zhang, and A. Harvey, Non-thermal plasma as a promising route for the removal of tar from the product gas of biomass gasification – A critical review. Chemical Engineering Journal, 2020. 382.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress and prospects in catalytic water treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. I. Parvulescu, F. Epron, H. Garcia, and P. Granger, Recent Progress and Prospects in Catalytic Water Treatment. Chem Rev, 2022. 122(3): p. 2981-3121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced oxidation processes in microreactors for water and wastewater treatment: development, challenges, and opportunities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Dong, B. Chen, B. Liu, L. J. Hounjet, Y. Cao, S. R. Stoyanov, et al., Advanced oxidation processes in microreactors for water and wastewater treatment: Development, challenges, and opportunities. Water Research, 2022. 211: p. 118047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Computational insight into complex structures of thorium coordination with N, N’- bis(3-allyl salicylidene)-o-phenylenediamine
